does: This paper demonstrates that reward models trained to assess human preferences can effectively detect text generated by aligned large language models. The core insight is that aligned LLMs, which are trained to maximize human preference signals through reinforcement learning from human feedback, produce text that receives higher preference scores from reward models compared to genuine human-written text. The authors propose two training schemes—continual preference fine-tuning and an additional approach that appears to be cut off in the abstract—to enhance the reward model's detection capability. The method leverages this systematic difference in preference scores as a detection signal, offering a unified approach to identify generations across multiple aligned LLMs without requiring model-specific detectors.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of several research streams: LLM-generated text detection methods, which have grown increasingly important as generative models proliferate; reward modeling and learning from human feedback, which have become central to aligning modern LLMs; and the study of artifacts introduced by alignment training. The paper connects to prior work on LGT detection that struggles with the diversity of LLM architectures and training procedures, as well as research documenting how RLHF-aligned models exhibit characteristic behaviors distinct from base models. It responds to the practical challenge that building individual detectors for each LLM is infeasible, proposing instead to target a common training procedure shared across state-of-the-art models.
enables: This approach opens several research directions: developing more sophisticated detection methods by analyzing the preference score distributions of various LLM types, investigating whether the preference-based signature persists across different reward models and alignment objectives, and exploring adversarial alignment procedures that might evade detection. The work could inform the design of future alignment methods that balance human preference maximization with detectability. It also enables broader detection systems for multimodal models or other domains where similar alignment procedures are applied. Additionally, the findings suggest future work on understanding whether preference-based artifacts are unavoidable consequences of RLHF alignment or can be mitigated through alternative training schemes.
